metadata.summary: Advanced python 'what changed and what do we need to do' tracking

documentation:
# pypipegraph2
[
![Docs available](https://badgen.net/badge/Docs/available/green?icon=docs)
](https://tyberiusprime.github.io/pypipegraph2/)
[![PyPI version](https://badge.fury.io/py/pypipegraph2.svg)](https://pypi.org/projects/pypipegraph2)
[
![Nix flake](https://badgen.net/badge/Nix%20flake/available/blue?icon=docs)
](https://github.com/TyberiusPrime/pypipegraph2/blob/main/flake.nix)

<svg width="93.8" height="20" viewBox="0 0 938 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="docs: available">
  <title>docs: available</title>
  <linearGradient id="FaUuN" x2="0" y2="100%">
    <stop offset="0" stop-opacity=".1" stop-color="#EEE"/>
    <stop offset="1" stop-opacity=".1"/>
  </linearGradient>
  <mask id="MaZyA"><rect width="938" height="200" rx="30" fill="#FFF"/></mask>
  <g mask="url(#MaZyA)">
    <rect width="350" height="200" fill="#555"/>
    <rect width="588" height="200" fill="#08C" x="350"/>
    <rect width="938" height="200" fill="url(#FaUuN)"/>
  </g>
  <g aria-hidden="true" fill="#fff" text-anchor="start" font-family="Verdana,DejaVu Sans,sans-serif" font-size="110">
    <text x="60" y="148" textLength="250" fill="#000" opacity="0.25">docs</text>
    <text x="50" y="138" textLength="250">docs</text>
    <text x="405" y="148" textLength="488" fill="#000" opacity="0.25">available</text>
    <text x="395" y="138" textLength="488">available</text>
  </g>
  
</svg>
[docs](https://tyberiusprime.github.io/pypipegraph2/)

Fine-grained tracking of what goes into generated artifacts,
and when it's necessary to recalculate them.

Also, trivial parallelization.



## Description

There's a bunch of 'pipeline' packages out there.

For scientific workflow management.

Often with a lot of 'magic'.

SnakeMake for example is popular.

Pypipegraph2 is a bit different.

You build a directed, acyclic graph (DAG) of jobs that you want done.

A job is, for example,

 * a python function and the name of the file(s) it generates.
 * a python function
 * a name and a set of parameters
 * the name of an input file


Pypipegraph2 will hash all the things. Your input files,
your (intermediary) output files. Your parameters. Even
your python functions (source and bytecode).

When any of those change, or the number of inputs to a job changes,
we recalculate it. Only if it's output changed, we recalculate the immediate downstreams.
Do they return their old output (because your change was incosequential)? Then we do not
recalculate their downstreams.

That's the big thing with respect to pypipegraph 1. When the input changed back then,
everything downstream was recalculated. 

You can use this in notebooks. You can use it in scripts. You can use it in
complicated scientific pipelines. It scales easily to a few 100.000 jobs.

Here is the simplest example:

```python
import pypipegraph2 as ppg2

ppg2.new()

def do_it(output_path):
	output_path.write_text("Hello world")
	
job = ppg2.FileGeneratingJob('hello.txt', do_it)
ppg2.run()
```

And you'll get two jobs: A FileGeneratingJob and a FunctionInvariant
to go with it.

If you change  `do_it`
and rerun your script, the output will change.

```
def do_it(output_path):
	output_path.write_text("Hello world, how are you today")
```

If you delete the output file, if you introduce a dependency, say by `job.depends_on(ppg.FunctionInvariant(my_other_function))`,
if you remove such a dependency, 'hello.txt' will be rebuild.


For interactive work, you can redefine jobs and rerun the same graph again.

If jobs fail, those downstream of them / dependent on them will not be evalutated.
But everything outside of that part of the DAG will be.

Jobs will run in parallel, using both multi-threading 
(for jobs modifying the currently running program) and multi-process (for FileGenerating
jobs).

Jobs like AttributeLoading, and TempFileGenerating have cleanups that run 
when their immediate downstreams have been processed. The also only run when 
they're required by a downstream, or when thier inputs have changed.

FunctionInvariants are *smart*. They compare bytecode if you're using the same 
python version, and fall back to source code if you have changed it.


# Jobs available


* `FileGeneratingJob(path, function)` -  generate one file
* `MultiFileGeneratingJob(paths | dict of paths, function)` -  generate multiple files
* `TempFileGeneratingJob(path, function)` -  generate one file, and remove it asap
* `MultiTempFileGeneratingJob(paths | dict of paths, function)` -  generate multiple files, and remove them asap
* `FunctionInvariant(name, function)` - track the source/bytecode of a function 
* FileInvariant(path) - track an input file
* `ParameterInvariant(name, (something, {'other': True}))` - track parameters
* `DataLoading(name, func)` - run this function in the current process
* `AttributeLoading(name, object, attribute_name, func)` - store the result of func on object
* `CachedDataLoading(path, calc_func, load_func)` - run func, cache the output of calc_func in a file, load_func(output) if required
* `AttributeLoading(path, object, attribute_name, func)` - store the result of func into a file, and load when necessary
* `JobGeneratingjob(name, func)` - generate more jobs (after the upstreams have run!)
* `PlotJob(output_path, calc_func, plot_func)` - generate some data, store it in a cache file, dump it a spreadsheet, generate a plot from the data, store it in output_path)
	
# Rust engine

Starting with version 3.0.0, the actual engine is written in Rust.

This is a complete rewrite of the inner workings. There were a small number of situations
left where a graph would not evaluate, mostly involving failing jobs, and the python solution
was very hard to follow - thanks to the 'run-on-demand' nature of temporary jobs.

The new rust engine is based on the insight that while externally, we have a lot of job classes,
for the evaluation only three kinds of jobs exists: Always, Output and Ephemeral.

This allowed us a much more complete testing regime, the engine was tested to evaluate with all possible
graphs (minus isomorphic equivalents) up to 7 nodes, and all possible graphs with 1..n failures
up to 6 nodes (and some quarter of the possible 7 node graphs). This has increased my confidence
into this implementation finally being correct.

The drawback of course is that you need to install a binary wheel, or build with maturin.
The nix-flake has a dev enviroment with everything setup.


## Note

Differences to pypipegraph

	- graphs can now be run multiple times
	- calling a job will run the graph cut down to this job and it's prerequisites.
	  Some jobs - like PlotJobs will return something useful from the call.

	- FileGeneratingJob-callbacks now must take the target filename as first parameter
	  MultiFileGeneratingJob receive either their mapping, or their list of output files.
	  This is being checked at definitaion time.
	- MultiFileGeneratingJob may receive a dict of 'pretty name' -> filename'. 
	  Then you can depend_on(mfg['pretty name A']) to only invalidate when 'filenameA's content changes!
	- PlotJob now returns a tuple: (PlotJob, Optional(CalcJob), Optional(TableJob).
	  This removes all the unintuitive ugliness of 'which job will depends_on add the
	  dependency to'.
	- PlotJob skip_table / skip_caching are now create_table and cache_calc (defaulting to True)
	- CachedAttributeLoadingJob now returns a tuple (AttributeLoadingJob, FileGeneratingJob)
	- .ignore_code_changes has been replaced by constructor argument depend_on_function (inverted!)
	- ppg.run_pipegraph() / ppg.new_pipegraph() is now just ppg.run()/ppg.ew()
	- ppg.RunTimeError is now ppg.RunFailed

	- Removed the following due to 'no usage':
		- class MemMappedDataLoadingJob
		- class DependencyInjectionJob 
		- class TempFilePlusGeneratingJob 
		- class NotebookJob
		- class CombinedPlotJob
		- class FinalJob (was only used in the Bil)
		- PlotJob.add_fiddle
		- class JobList (depends_on handles all use cases without this special class / and it was unused)

		
	- a failed jobs exceptions are no longer available as job.exception,
      they can now be found in ppg.global_pipegraph.last_run[job_id].error
	  (last_run is also the result of ppg.run() if you set do_raise = False)

	- CycleError is now NotADag
	- ParameterInvariant no longer take an 'accept_as_unchanged' function. I suppose it would be trivial to implement using compare_hashes, but I couldn't find any current usage.
	- job + job (which returned a JobList) is no longer supported. Job.depends_on can be called with any number of jobs (this was already true in ppg1, but the + syntax was still around)
	- job.is_in_dependency_chain has been removed
	- the 'graph dumping' functionality has been removed for now
	- passing the wrong type of argument (such as a non callable to FunctionInvariant) raises TypeError instead of ValueError
	- FileGeneratingJob by default reject empty outputs (this can be changed with empty_ok=True) as a parameter. The default is inversed for MultiFileGeneratingJob.
	- In ppg1 if a file existed, a (new) FileGeneratingJob covering it was not run. PPG2 runs the FileGeneratingJob in order to record the right hash.
	- A failing job does no longer remove it's output. We know to rerun it because we didn't record it's new input hashes. This also means the rename_broken has been removed
	- TempFileGeneratingJob.do_cleanup_if_was_never_run is no more - I don't think it was ever used outside of testing
	- Defining multiple jobs creating the same output raises JobOutputConflict (more specific than ValueError)
	- The execution of 'useless' leaf jobs now usually happens at least once, due to them being invalidated by their FunctionInvariant
	- JobDiedException is now called just 'JobDied'
	- The various 'FileTimeInvariant/FileChecksumInvariant/RobustFileChecksumInvariant' forwarders have been removed. Use FileInvariant.
	- MultiFileInvariant is gone. Adding/removing FileInvariants now triggers by itself, no need to stuff multiple into a MultiFileInvariant
	- Pruning + running will no longer set ._pruned=pruning_job_id on downstream jobs, but .pruned_reason=pruning_job_id. Otherwise you could not unprune() and run again.
	- ppg.util.global_pipegraph is now ppg.global_pipegraph
	- Redefining a job in an incompatible way now raises JobRedefinitionError (instead of JobContractError)
	- Calling the same PlotJob once with cache_calc/create_table = True and once with False no longer triggers an exception, even in strict (RunMode.CONSOLE) mode. The jobs do stick around though.
	- interactive console mode
		- restart/reboot is now 'again/stop_and_again' to make it clearer
		- better progression, nice output 
		- some barely used commands were removed for now (runtimes (see log/runtimes.tsv), next, stay, errors, spy, spy-flame
	
	- new job kind: SharedMultiFileGeneratingJob
      This job's output folder is keyed by a hash of it's inputs, and can be easily shared between multiple ppgs from multiple places (replaces mbf_externals.PreBuildJob, conceptually)

from . import fixtures

documentation: ppg1 compatibility

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.testing.RaisesDirectOrInsidePipegraph
documentation:
Piece of black magic from the depths of _pytest
that will check whether a piece of code will raise the
expected expcition (if outside of ppg), or if it will
raise the exception when the pipegraph is running

Use as a context manager like pytest.raises

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.exceptions.PPGException
documentation: Baseclass for all our exceptions

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.exceptions.FatalGraphException
documentation: Could not finish executing the graph completly

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.exceptions.NotADag
documentation: Your graph is not acyclic

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.exceptions.JobOutputConflict
documentation: Multiple jobs with overlapping (but not identical) outputs were defined

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.exceptions.JobContractError
documentation: A job did not do what it was supposed to do

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.exceptions.JobEvaluationFailed
documentation: For some reason, we could not decide on whether to execute this job

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.exceptions.RunFailed
documentation: The execution failed outside of the scope of a single job

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.exceptions.JobsFailed
documentation: one or more jobs failed

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.exceptions.RunFailedInternally
documentation: There is a bug in pypipegraph2

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.exceptions._RunAgain
documentation: Internal to signal rerun

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.exceptions.JobError
documentation: Wrapper around the exceptions thrown by random jobs

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.entry_points.cli_record_as_generated_file
documentation:
Record <filename> (relative path) as if it was
done by a filegenerating job - provided it wasn't recorded
previously, and it had no input.

This should not be necessary in almost all circumstances.

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.ppg_traceback.Trace.__init__
documentation:
Extract traceback information.

Args:
    exc_type (Type[BaseException]): Exception type.
    exc_value (BaseException): Exception value.
    traceback (TracebackType): Python Traceback object.

Returns:
    Trace: A Trace instance which you can use to construct a `Traceback`.

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.ppg_traceback.Trace._format_rich_traceback_fallback
documentation:
Pretty print a traceback.

We don't use rich's own facility, since it is
not time-bounded /  does not cut the output

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.testing.fixtures.no_pipegraph
documentation: No pipegraph, but seperate directory per test

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.testing.fixtures.both_ppg_and_no_ppg
documentation:
Create both an inside and an outside ppg test case.
don't forgot to add this to your conftest.py

Use togother with run_ppg and force_load

```
def pytest_generate_tests(metafunc):
    if "both_ppg_and_no_ppg" in metafunc.fixturenames:
        metafunc.parametrize("both_ppg_and_no_ppg", [True, False], indirect=True)
```

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.util.CPUs
documentation: Detects the number of CPUs on a system. Cribbed from pp.

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.util.job_or_filename
documentation:
Take a filename, or a job. Return Path(filename), dependency-for-that-file
ie. either the job, or a invariant_class (default: FileInvariant)

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.util.assert_uniqueness_of_object
documentation:
Makes certain there is only one object with this class & .name.

This is necessary so the pipegraph jobs assign their data only to the
objects you're actually working with.

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.util.flatten_jobs
documentation:
Take an arbitrary deeply nested list of lists of jobs
and return just the jobs

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.util.pretty_log_errors
documentation:
capture exceptions (on a function outside of ppg)
and format it with our fancy local logging exception logger

This is a decorator!

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.util.wrap_for_function_invariant
documentation:
When you need to pass a function + parameters which ends up in a FunctionInvariant.
Think lambda: some_func(filename).

This marks it so that the FunctionInvariant tracks 'somefunc',
not the lambda.

class_variables.name: python.pypipegraph2.enums.JobOutcome.NotYet
documentation: default

class_variables.name: python.pypipegraph2.enums.JobOutcome.Failed
documentation: todo rename to Failure

class_variables.name: python.pypipegraph2.enums.JobOutcome.UpstreamFailed
documentation: rename to UpstreamFailure

documentation: certain redefinitions: FatalGraphException, interactive console, ctrl-c does not work

class_variables.name: python.pypipegraph2.enums.RunMode.CONSOLE_INTERACTIVE
documentation: certain redefinitions: warning. interactive console, ctrl-c does not work

documentation: in this process

modules.name: python.pypipegraph2
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.new
documentation:
create a new pipegraph.
You may pass reuse_last_or_default to all values
to reuse the last value,
or default to use the true default

(or load defaults)


Log retention is how many old logs (+ the current one) we
keep.

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.change_global_pipegraph
documentation: Helper to swap out the global pipegraph from ppg1-compability.util

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.replace_ppg1
documentation:
Turn all ppg1 references into actual ppg2
objects.
Best effort, but the commonly used API should be well supported.
Try to do this before anything imports ppg1.


One notably exception is in cores_needed/use_cores,
where ppg2 only supports 1/almost_all/all, and ppg1 also supported
a number of cores > 1. This get's convert to almost_all (-1 in ppg1 parlance)

Also jobs often throw TypeError, instead of ValueError if you pass the arguments
in the wrong order. This shouldn't affect any working code though.

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.unreplace_ppg1
documentation: undo replace_ppg1.

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.interactive.ConsoleInteractive.stop
documentation: Called from the runner

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.interactive.ConsoleInteractive._cmd_help
documentation: print help

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.interactive.ConsoleInteractive._cmd_default
documentation: print the currently running jobs (mapped to enter)

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.interactive.ConsoleInteractive._cmd_abort
documentation: Kill current jobs and exit (safely) asap

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.interactive.ConsoleInteractive._cmd_die
documentation: kill the current process without saving history

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.interactive.ConsoleInteractive._cmd_stop
documentation: Exit after current jobs finished

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.interactive.ConsoleInteractive._cmd_again
documentation: Restart the current python program after all jobs have completed

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.interactive.ConsoleInteractive._cmd_kill
documentation: kill a running job (by id)

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.interactive.ConsoleInteractive._cmd_debug
documentation: Write the debug status from the evaluator to debug.txt

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.ppg1_compatibility.replace_ppg1
documentation:
Turn all ppg1 references into actual ppg2
objects.
Best effort, but the commonly used API should be well supported.
Try to do this before anything imports ppg1.

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.ppg1_compatibility.unreplace_ppg1
documentation:
Turn ppg1 compatibility layer off, restoring ppg1
not that well tested, I suppose...

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.ppg1_compatibility.wrap_job
documentation: Adapt for ppg1 api idiosyncracies

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.ppg1_compatibility.run_pipegraph
documentation: Run the current global pipegraph

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.ppg1_compatibility._first_param_empty
documentation:
Check whether the first argument to this call is
empty, ie. no with a default value

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.ppg1_compatibility.CachedAttributeLoadingJob
documentation: no one inherits from these, so wrapping in a function is ok, I suppose
they should have been functions in ppg1.e..

variables.name: python.pypipegraph2.ppg1_compatibility.invariant_status_filename_default
documentation: pragma: no cover

variables.name: python.pypipegraph2.ppg1_compatibility.invariant_status_filename_default
documentation: pragma: no cover

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.ppg1_compatibility.Util.checksum_file
documentation: was used by outside functions

modules.name: python.pypipegraph2.cli
documentation: CLI interface to ppg2

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.cli.main_filter_constraint_violations
documentation:
For when you really need to remove some jobs from the pipegraph's history.
Takes a filename with job ids.
Defaults to .ppg/errors/latest/constraint_violations.jobs

If you have your ppg history in a non standard place, pass in a ppg
with the correct directories set.

modules.name: python.pypipegraph2.parallel
documentation: Parlallel computation / threading support helpers

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.parallel.async_raise
documentation:
Raises an asynchronous exception in another thread.
Read <http://docs.python.org/c-api/init.html#PyThreadState_SetAsyncExc>
for further enlightenments.
\:param target_tid: target thread identifier
\:param exception: Exception class to be raised in that thread

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.parallel.CoreLock
documentation:
Allow threads to request n 'cores',
if they're available, let it proceed.
If they're not available, block.
If they exceed the maxmimum number available: raise.

Essentially, this is a Semaphore with multi-resource-one-step-acquisition.

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.hashers.hash_file
documentation:
delegate to a fast and somewhat collision resistant hash function.
Or reuse a .sha256 file if present and at least as new as the file.
(Having one present that's older than the file is a JobContractError).
The file must contain exactly one 64 character hash (+- a newline)

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.graph.PyPipeGraph.run
documentation: Run the complete pypipegraph

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.graph.PyPipeGraph._run
documentation:
Run the jobgraph - possibly focusing on a subset of jobs (ie. ignoring
anything that's not necessary to calculate them - activated by calling a Job

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.graph.PyPipeGraph.run_for_these
documentation: Run graph for just these jobs (and their upstreams), ignoring everything else

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.graph.PyPipeGraph._cleanup_logs
documentation: Clean up old logs

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.graph.PyPipeGraph._cleanup_errors
documentation: Cleanup old errors and drop a 'latest' symlink

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.graph.PyPipeGraph._log_runtimes
documentation: Log the runtimes to a file (ever growing. But only runtimes over a threshold)

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.graph.PyPipeGraph.get_history_filename
documentation: where do we store the graph's history?

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.graph.PyPipeGraph._convert_old_history
documentation:
Convert pre-rust history,
by assuming that it's the same graph, and you changed *nothing* else in the upgrade

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.graph.PyPipeGraph._resolve_dependency_callbacks
documentation:
jobs may depend on functions that return their actual dependencies.
This resolves them

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.graph.PyPipeGraph._install_signals
documentation:
make sure we don't crash just because the user logged of.
Also blocks CTRl-c in console, and transaltes into save shutdown otherwise.

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.graph.PyPipeGraph._restore_signals
documentation: Restore signals to pre-run values

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.graph.PyPipeGraph.add
documentation:
Add a job.
Automatically called when a Job() is created

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.graph.PyPipeGraph.add_edge
documentation:
Declare a dependency between jobs

Implementation note:
While we connect the DAG based on the Jobs,
we calculate invalidation based on the job_inputs (see Job.depends_on),
allowing a MultiFileGeneratingJob to (optionally) only invalidate some of it's downstreams.

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.graph.PyPipeGraph.has_edge
documentation: Does this edge already exist?

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.graph.PyPipeGraph.restart_afterwards
documentation:
Restart the whole python program afterwards?
Used by the interactive console

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.graph.PyPipeGraph.dump_subgraph_for_debug
documentation:
Write a subgraph_debug.py
with a faked-out version of this graph.
See Job.dump_subgraph_for_debug for details

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.graph.PyPipeGraph.dump_subgraph_for_debug_at_run
documentation:
Write a subgraph_debug.py
with a faked-out version of this graph.
See Job.dump_subgraph_for_debug for details.

This version dumps *after* the cleanup jobs
and so on have been assembled (ie. when run is called)

and so on have been assembled (ie. when run is called)

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.runner.spawn_watcher
documentation:
The watcher registers to be informed when the parent process dies.
It then goes and kills the process group,
which we use to reliably tag the spawned processes.

That means you can't spawn a daemon process from a ppg job.

The watcher also get's a sig_int when the graph execution ends,
which also signals it to go and terminate all stragglers.

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.runner.Runner

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.runner.Runner.modify_dag
documentation:
Modify the DAG to be executed
by pruning

    - removing jobs we ran in the last run-through

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.runner.Runner._start_job_executing_threads

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.runner.Runner._start_another_thread
documentation:
Fire up another thread (if all current threads are blocked with multi core threads.

This prevents stalling, since it will ensure that there's a thread around
to do the SingleCore jobs.

Note that we don't fire up threads without limit - at one point, you can still
stall the graph

Run a given JobGraph

only in place during the dag modification step of Runner.__init__,
so that the jobs that are only created during run (cleanup, )
do not end up in the actual graph.

substantial difference is that DataLoadingJobs run when their downstream needs them,

methods.name: tests.test_job_generating_jobs.TestJobGeneratingJob.test_job_gen_leading_to_missing_history
documentation:
test_job_gen_leading_to_missing_history

A case where our previous 'trash history when MFG is renamed'
functionality lead to throwing away of history.
this test only works if you'd remove the locking around next_job_number
*and* add in a small delay to actually trigger it
def test_massive_trying_to_hit_jobnumber_conflict(self):
ppg.new(cores=4)
def fail(of):
raise ValueError()
def jj(prefix):
def inner():
Path(prefix).mkdir(exist_ok=True)
for i in range(0,100):
ppg.FileGeneratingJob(prefix + '/' + str(i), lambda of: of.write_text(prefix))
return inner
a = ppg.JobGeneratingJob('a', jj('a'))
b = ppg.JobGeneratingJob('b', jj('b'))
c = ppg.JobGeneratingJob('c', jj('c'))
d = ppg.JobGeneratingJob('d', jj('d'))
ppg.run()

methods.name: tests.test_job_generating_jobs.TestJobGeneratingJob.test_job_gen_leading_to_missing_history_part2
documentation:
test_job_gen_leading_to_missing_history_part2(

A case where our previous 'trash history when MFG is renamed'
functionality lead to throwing away of history.

This does the same, but verifies we keep the history when the gen job is not using the MFG

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs._mark_function_wrapped
documentation:
mark a function as 'wrapped' - ie. the FunctionInvariant
is being created on the callback, but what the graph calls is outer
(which is supposed to call inner!)

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.PlotJob
documentation:
Return a tuple of 3 jobs, the last two entries might be none.

The first one is always a FileGeneratingJob
    around a wrapped plot_function, creating the output filename.

If cache_calc is set, the second one is a CachedAttributeLoadingJob
(wich again is a tuple, load_job, calc_job),
loading a .data_ member on the first job returned.

If create_table is set, the third one is a FileGeneratingJob
writing (output_filename + '.tsv').

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.NotebookJob
documentation:
Run a jupyter notebook, tracking input and output files.
Then notebook is rendered into an html file in html_output_folder
Reruns whenever the *code* of the notebook changes,
not the output results.

functions.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.ExternalJob
documentation:
A job that calls an external program,
logging the command, stdout & stderr to files
in @output_path (directory!).

If the process creates additional files that you want to track,
add them as dictionary in @additional_created_files.
The values are relative to output_path!
(job.files has them resolved.)

If you have an external program that needs to run multiple times in the same
output folder, you can use std_prefix to distinguish the
stderr/stdout/cmd/returncode files.

The @cmd_or_cmd_func may be a callback - in that case it's called once,
when the job is actually executed, allowing you to access all dependencies.

It may contain ExternalOutputPath objects, which are resolved to the output_path,
and passed as str(path.absolute()) to your called process

If the command returns something else than 0 on success,
adjust allowed_return_codes.

use @call_before(job) and @call_after(job) for pre/postprocessing.
Use job.files[key] to access our output files
(default keys are 'stdout', 'stderr', 'cmd' and 'return_code').

You can create tempfile generating jobs by adjusting the job_cls to
ppg.TempFileGeneratingJob.

The working dir of the called process is by default the output_path,
but can be overwritten with cwd.

The @output_path is stored in job.output_path

@start_new_session is passed on to subprocess.Popen

variables.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.deep_diff_too_old_for_dataframes
documentation: doesn't matter

variables.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.python_version
documentation: we only care about major.minor

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.JobList
documentation:
Jobs.depends_on already takes any iterable of jobs.
But sometimes you want to say [jobs,...].depends_on,
and this allows that.

For ppg1 compatibility

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.Job.__iter__
documentation: It yields self so you can use jobs and list of jobs uniformly

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.Job.cleanup
documentation: Overwritten by Ephemeral jobs downstream

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.Job.readd
documentation:
Readd this job to the current global pipegraph
(possibly the *new* global pipegraph).
Without any dependencies!

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.Job.depends_on
documentation:
Depend on another Job, which must be done before this one can run.
If the other job changes it's output, this job will be invalidated (and rerun).

You may pass in one ore more Jobs, a list of such,
ore a callable that will return such. The callable will
be called when the ppg is run the first time.
(This is usefull if you need to finalize e.g. a tree structure
 and design the actual dependencies on that tree structure).

This method will also take a filename, in which case we'll look
up the job producing the filename.
Bonus: Dependency is the nonly on the contens of that file,
not on all files of the (MultiFileGenerating) Job.

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.Job.extract_strict_hash
documentation:
Our regular 'hashes' contain things
like mtime/size, per-python-bytecode etc
to allow flexible compare_hashes.

For the Shared jobs, we need something that
is strictly a set of bytes to compare.

Note that we call this with only a single output hash.

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.Job.depends_on_func
documentation:
Create a function invariant.
Return a NamedTumple (invariant, self)
(so you can change with job.depends_on_func(func)[1].depends_on(...))

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.Job.__call__
documentation:
execute just enough of the pipeline that this job get's evaluated.
A job may return something here for interactive work (plot jobs for example
will return the plot object

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.Job.exception
documentation:
Interrogate global pipegraph for this job's exception.
Mostly for the ppg1 tests...

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.Job.failed
documentation:
Did this job fail in any way in the last run?
I.e. does it have an exception?

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.Job.stack_trace
documentation:
Interrogate global pipegraph for this job's exception stacktrace.
Mostly for the ppg1 tests...

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.Job.upstreams
documentation:
Return a list of jobs that are directly upstream of this one by querying the
global pipegraph

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.Job.dump_subgraph_for_debug
documentation:
Take every job leading up to this job
and write a mock dependency graph to
subgraph_debug.py

Very handy for debugging, but ugly :).

Note that you can just throw out Jobs later,
the edges of non-existant jobs will be ignored.

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.MultiFileGeneratingJob._do_inside_fork
documentation: def callback(self):
self.generating_function(*self.get_input())

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.MultiFileGeneratingJob.annotate
documentation: Delgate to ppg2 to annotate a job's output with markdown strings, just like ppg2-watcher would do

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.FileGeneratingJob.annotate
documentation: Delgate to ppg2 to annotate a job's output with markdown strings, just like ppg2-watcher would do

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.MultiTempFileGeneratingJob
documentation:
A job that creates files that are removed
once all it's dependents have run.

These will always run at least once,
(due to the 'virtual clean up jobs' capturing the dependencies)

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs._InputHashAwareJobMixin

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs._InputHashAwareJobMixin._derive_output_name
documentation: Given the set of inputs, get as the key for by_input

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs._InputHashAwareJobMixin._hash_hashes
documentation:
The problem at this point is that the hashes
are not strictly what-the-input-depends-on
but also things we use to
omit recalculating (expensive) hashes if possible,
e.g. size/mtime for FileInvariants or
per-python-version-bytecodes for FunctionInvariants.

We have to extract just the relevant data,
and for that we need to lookup the actual jobs.
Jobs that can examine the hashes of what went into them

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs._FunctionInvariant.get_source
documentation: Return the 'source' and whether this was a python function

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs._FunctionInvariant._get_source_from_non_python_function
documentation: get source for built ins, cython, etc

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs._FunctionInvariant.extract_closure
documentation: extract the bound variables from a function into a string representation

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs._FunctionInvariant.dis_code
documentation:
'dissassemble' python code.
Strips lambdas (they change address every execution otherwise),
but beginning with 3.7 these are actually included

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs._FunctionInvariant.get_cython_source
documentation:
Attempt to get the cython source for a function.
Requires cython code to be compiled with -p or #embed_pos_in_docstring=True in the source file

Unfortunatly, finding the right module (to get an absolute file path) is not straight forward,
we inspect all modules in sys.module, and their children, but we might be missing sub-sublevel modules,
in which case we'll need to increase search depth

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs._FunctionInvariant.func_to_name
documentation: Automatically derive a name for a function

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.ParameterInvariant.freeze
documentation:
Turn dicts into tuples of (key,value),
lists into tuples, and sets
into frozensets, recursively - useful
to get a hash value..

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.ValuePlusHash
documentation: Wrapper to signal AttributeLoading/DataLoadingJob that you have already calculated a hash on this

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.UseInputHashesForOutput
documentation: Wrapper to signal AttributeLoadingJob/DataLoadingJob that you want to use the input hashes for the output hash

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.DataLoadingJob
documentation:
A job that manipulates the currently running python program.

Note that these don't run if they have no dependents.

Also note that if this runs, and the load_function returns None,
it *will* invalidate it's downstreams, possibly triggering reruns
you did not want.

The answer is to return something that's we can feed to
hasher.hash_bytes - bytes or a string.

Alternatively, if you have a hash handy, you may return it wrapped in
an ValuePlusHash.

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.JobGeneratingJob
documentation:
A job generating job runs once per ppg.Graph.run(),
and may alter the graph in essentially any way. The changes are ignored
until the first run finishes, then the whole graph is rerun.

This has has to run every time to actually create its downstream jobs,
for example when the first pipegraph run crashed,
and you're rerunning the whole program.

If you depend on a JobGeneratingJob your job will be invalidated
every time the JobGeneratingJob runs.

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.SharedMultiFileGeneratingJob

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.SharedMultiFileGeneratingJob._handle_function_dependency
documentation: Unlike the other jobs, SharedMultiFileGeneratingJob do not 'merge' their FunctionInvariants when possible

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.SharedMultiFileGeneratingJob.target_folder
documentation:
read the target folder as of the last ppg run,
so you can find your actual files

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.SharedMultiFileGeneratingJob._log_local_usage
documentation:
Write the input key we used to a log file,
so that non-ppg-interactive stuff may read it back
and find the files

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.SharedMultiFileGeneratingJob._cleanup
documentation: Remove outputs / symlinks that have no longer entries in the used folder

A shared MultiFileGeneratingJob.

Sharing means that this can be produced by multiple pypipegraphs,
each with it's own history.

The trick here is that we hash the inputs into one hash,
by which we name the target directory.

But that's actually a symlink to another directory,
named by the output hashes!

That way, changing the input will trigger a recalc.
But we will not store the output multiple times
if it's identical.

Multiple graphs, with the same input hashes,
will not do the work twice. (Provided their runtimes don't overlap).
Otherwise calc will happen twice, but one thrown away ( if identical )
or with an exception ( if not identical: input-defines-output invariant violated).

Broken builds get removed by default.

Each pipegraph also logs it's use (on first usage & rebuild) and by default
cleans up no longer used outputs / symlinks.

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.NotebookInvariant
documentation: An invariant that checks only the code and markdown from a notebook (not the results)

classes.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.ExternalOutputPath

methods.name: python.pypipegraph2.jobs.ExternalOutputPath.__truediv__
documentation: implement /

This allows you to refer to the output path
in your ExternalJob commands in a don't-need-to-know-the -path way
Just plug cmd = [..., ExternalOutputPath / "output.txt",...]
in there

methods.name: tests.ppg1_compatibility_layer.test_invariants_and_depedencies.TestInvariantPPG1.test_invariant_loading_issues_on_key
documentation: ppg2_rust
def test_invariant_loading_issues_on_value_undepickableclass(self):
import tempfile
import pickle
ppg.util.global_pipegraph.quiet = False
# make sure Undepickable is Undepickable
with tempfile.TemporaryFile("wb+") as tf:
o = Undepickable()
pickle.dump(o, tf, pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL)
with pytest.raises(pickle.UnpicklingError):
tf.seek(0, 0)
pickle.load(tf)
a = ppg.ParameterInvariant("a", 5)
b = ppg.FileGeneratingJob("out/b", lambda: write("out/b", "b"))
b.ignore_code_changes()
c = ppg.ParameterInvariant("c", 23)
b.depends_on(a)
write("out/b", "a")
# ppg2
ppg.util.global_pipegraph.get_history_filename().parent.mkdir(
exist_ok=True, parents=True
with gzip.GzipFile(
ppg.util.global_pipegraph.get_history_filename(), "wb"
) as op:
pickle.dump(a.job_id, op, pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL)
pickle.dump(Undepickable(), op, pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL)
pickle.dump(c.job_id, op, pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL)
pickle.dump(
({"a": 23}, {"c": 23}), op, pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL
)  # ppg2 expects inputs, outputs
with pytest.raises(ppg.RuntimeError):
ppg.run_pipegraph()
assert read("out/b") == "b"  # job was run
assert a.job_id in ppg.util.global_pipegraph.invariant_loading_issues
assert ppg.util.global_pipegraph._load_history()["PIc"] == (

methods.name: tests.test_basics.TestPypipegraph2.test_two_temp_jobs
documentation:
test_two_temp_jobs
This tests one of the 'unnecessary' temp job reruns.
We have these jobs
Fi:TA -> TA -> C
               ^
Fi:TB -> TB    ->  D

(todo: this full argument might have been invalidated
by the non-transitive-hull changes?)

which means, after the graph rewriting,
TA and TB depend on each other's FunctionInvariants
(TempJobs steal the invariants from their downstreams,
so that whenever the downstream is triggered,
they are as well, and before hand.)

If now Fi:TB triggers, we must recalculate TB,
and we also recalculate TA.
But if TB does not not lead to C and D's invalidation,
we have recalculated TA unnecessarily.

But I can't figure out a better way to do it.
Handling TempJobs by anything other than graph rewriting has
proven to be an absolute mess of a conditional event loop that
I'm not capable of cutting through.

The graph rewriting is elegant and makes the do-the-jobs event loop
almost trivial. It fails on this particular task though.
Not that it is given that a back-and-forth graph walking approach
(ie. when C is triggered, go back and (re)do TA) would be able to
actually avoid the issue.

documentation: this test is fairly unreliable with shifting pandas
and python versions - and it's a nieche case anyway.
def test_no_source_traceback(self):
def a(of):
import pandas
df = pandas.DataFrame()
df["shu"]  # which should raise
ppg.FileGeneratingJob("a", a)
with pytest.raises(ppg.JobsFailed):
ppg.run()
ppg.global_pipegraph.dir_config.error_dir
/ ppg.global_pipegraph.time_str
/ "0_exception.txt"
).read_text()
# other versions have the source.
assert "# no source available" in e
assert "KeyError" in e
